title: Two States Are Suing the Largest Cord Blood Bank for Lying to New Parents
description: Texas and Arizona are suing Cord Blood Registry for claiming its $5,000 service can treat '80+ conditions.' Doctors say that's not true — and a million families may have been misled.
lede: When you're holding your newborn for the first time, a company tells you there's a 'once-in-a-lifetime opportunity' to bank their cord blood — and it could someday save their life. Two state attorneys general say that's a lie.

sections.heading: The Lawsuit
paragraphs: Texas and Arizona have filed separate lawsuits against Cord Blood Registry (CBR), the largest private cord blood bank in the United States. CBR stores more than one million samples of umbilical cord cells in its Tucson, Arizona facility, charging families thousands of dollars in upfront and annual storage fees.

The company's website claims that storing cord blood is a 'once-in-a-lifetime opportunity' because the cells have the 'potential to treat 80+ conditions.' Both state attorneys general allege these claims are false and constitute deceptive advertising under consumer protection law.

Arizona Attorney General Kris Mayes put it bluntly: 'We know that so many families were deceived by this company at a time when they are in their most vulnerable moment.' The lawsuits seek to force CBR to remove the misleading advertisements and compensate families who were duped.

sections.heading: What Cord Blood Banking Actually Does
paragraphs: Cord blood — the blood remaining in the umbilical cord after birth — does contain stem cells that can be used in certain medical treatments, particularly bone marrow transplants for blood cancers and some genetic disorders. Public cord blood banks, which collect donations for anyone who needs them, have genuine medical value.

Private cord blood banking is a different story. When you pay CBR or a similar company to store your baby's cord blood, you're betting that your child will someday need their own stem cells for treatment. Medical experts say this almost never happens. The odds of a child needing their own privately banked cord blood are estimated at 1 in 20,000 to 1 in 200,000.

The American Academy of Pediatrics does not recommend private cord blood banking for most families. The organization has stated that private banking should only be considered when there is a known medical condition in the family that could benefit from stem cell transplantation — a very narrow set of circumstances.

sections.heading: The Predatory Pregnancy Industry
paragraphs: Cord blood banking is part of a larger pattern of predatory marketing that targets expecting and new parents. From unnecessary genetic tests to unproven supplements to fear-based birth planning services, the pregnancy industry generates billions by exploiting parental anxiety.

What makes cord blood banking particularly insidious is the timing: the sales pitch is delivered when parents are overwhelmed, emotional, and terrified of making a mistake. Saying 'no' to banking your baby's cord blood feels like saying 'no' to potentially saving their life — even when the actual medical evidence doesn't support the claim.

The best defense against predatory marketing during pregnancy is evidence-based information. Understanding your reproductive health, knowing what your cycle actually tells you, and being able to distinguish real medical recommendations from marketing claims can save thousands of dollars and enormous emotional stress.

sections.heading: What New Parents Should Actually Focus On
paragraphs: Instead of spending $2,000-5,000 on private cord blood banking, medical experts recommend considering public cord blood donation (free, and it helps patients who actually need stem cells), establishing a relationship with a trusted pediatrician, and focusing on evidence-based prenatal and postnatal care.

For those trying to conceive, understanding your fertility window and cycle health with evidence-based tools is far more valuable than premium products marketed through fear. Free, private, browser-based calculators can provide the same information that fertility clinics charge hundreds for during initial consultations.

The cord blood banking lawsuits are a reminder that during pregnancy and early parenthood, 'premium' doesn't always mean 'better.' Sometimes the most helpful tools are the simplest ones — and the most expensive ones are the most misleading.

faqs.q: Is private cord blood banking worth the money?
faqs.a: For most families, no. The American Academy of Pediatrics does not recommend private cord blood banking unless there's a known medical condition in the family. The odds of a child needing their own banked cord blood are 1 in 20,000 to 1 in 200,000. Public donation is a free alternative that helps others.

faqs.q: Why are Texas and Arizona suing Cord Blood Registry?
faqs.a: Both states allege that CBR made false advertising claims, including that cord blood can treat '80+ conditions' and that banking is a 'once-in-a-lifetime opportunity.' Medical experts say these claims are misleading and overstate the current medical utility of privately banked cord blood.

faqs.q: How much does private cord blood banking cost?
faqs.a: Private cord blood banking typically costs $1,500-2,500 for collection and initial processing, plus $100-300 per year in annual storage fees. Over 18 years, the total cost can exceed $5,000.

faqs.q: What's the difference between public and private cord blood banking?
faqs.a: Public banks collect cord blood donations that are available to anyone who needs a stem cell transplant — this has proven medical value. Private banks store cord blood exclusively for the donor family, but the chance of ever using it is extremely low (1 in 20,000+).

faqs.q: What should I focus on instead of cord blood banking?
faqs.a: Medical experts recommend public cord blood donation (free), establishing early pediatric care, evidence-based prenatal health monitoring, and understanding your reproductive cycle with validated tools rather than spending on premium pregnancy products.
